topic: village elder gave advice on pruning old trees

turns.content: my friend I have a question about pruning. one of the elders in the village told me I should cut back my old cocoa trees very hard. almost to the trunk. he says they will come back stronger. is this true

What the elder is describing sounds like what's known as "coppicing" or heavy rejuvenation pruning. It is a real technique used on old, low-yielding cocoa trees, but it comes with both benefits and risks.

**Benefits:** Old trees that are barely producing can regenerate new shoots from the trunk. These new branches can produce well for another 10-15 years.

**Risks:** Not all old trees survive such a hard cut. If the tree is diseased or the trunk is hollow, cutting it back hard may kill it. You also lose 1-2 years of production while the new growth establishes.

How old are the trees he's talking about? And are they still producing, even a little?

some of my oldest trees. they are producing but not much. maybe 10 pods where the younger trees give 40 or 50. the trunks are thick and strong. no disease. but the branches are tangled and growing into each other

If the trunks are thick, strong, and disease-free, those are good candidates for rejuvenation pruning. The tangled, overcrowded branches are exactly the problem — they shade each other out and the tree wastes energy maintaining old wood instead of producing pods.

You have two options:

1. **Full coppice** (what the elder suggests): cut the trunk down to about 1 metre height. The tree sends up vigorous new shoots. High risk, high reward. You lose all production from that tree for 1-2 years.

2. **Staged pruning**: cut back about one-third of the canopy each year over three years. Less shock to the tree. You keep some production while the new growth fills in.

Given that these trees are still giving you 10 pods, I'd lean toward the staged approach. But the elder's experience matters too. Has he done this himself?
he did it on his farm many years ago. he says all the trees came back. but he is old now and his memory may add a few trees that did not survive. hah. I respect him but I must think carefully

Ha — memory does have a way of improving the results over time. That is wise of you to weigh his advice carefully.

Here is what I would suggest: **start with two or three trees only**. Choose the ones producing the least. Try the full coppice on one and the staged pruning on another. See which responds better over the next year.

That way you are not risking everything on one approach. You are testing. If both methods work, you can do more the following year with confidence.
that is sensible. test first then decide. my father used to say the man who tastes the soup before serving it never burns his guests

Your father's proverb is exactly right for this situation. Three trees is a small price for the knowledge you will gain.

When you choose which trees to test on, pick ones near the edge of the farm rather than the middle. If they fail, you haven't created a gap in the middle of your canopy where the other trees depend on each other for shade.
yes the edge is better. and if the new shoots come they will get more light at the edge too. when should I make the cuts. does the season matter

Timing matters a lot. The best time is **just before the rains start**, when moisture is coming but the ground is not yet soaking wet. That way:

- The tree has moisture to push out new shoots.
- The wounds dry slightly in the air before the heavy rains, which reduces fungal infection.

If heavy rains are already falling, the wet wounds attract disease. If it's too dry, the tree may not have the energy to regenerate.

Are the rains showing signs of coming in your area?
we had some light rain last week. not much but the clouds are building. I think the season is near. so this is a good time to cut then

Yes, this is ideal timing. Light rain with more coming means the trees will have moisture to support new growth right after the shock of pruning.

A few practical tips for the cuts:

- **Use a clean, sharp cutlass or saw.** Ragged cuts invite disease.
- **Cut at an angle** so water runs off the wound instead of sitting on it.
- **Mark the trees** so you remember which one you coppiced and which one you staged. You want to compare them properly.
I will tie a red cloth on the coppiced one and a blue cloth on the staged one. simple but it works. I will tell the elder his advice has a new student. he will like that

Red and blue — simple and clear. That is a farmer's system, no technology needed.

The elder will be proud. An old man likes to know his knowledge is not wasted. And you are honouring him the right way — by listening carefully and then acting wisely.
when you plant a tree you are making a promise to your grandchildren. these old trees were my grandfather's promise to me. I must take care of them. medaase my friend
That is a beautiful way to see it. Rejuvenation pruning is keeping that promise alive for another generation. Come back and tell me how those three trees respond. I would like to hear. Good evening.
